title: Diverse Indigenous nations flourish
summary: First Nations, Inuit and Métis histories predate Canada by millennia.
what: Complex societies developed distinct political systems, economies, languages and relationships with the land.
why: Treaty rights, sovereignty and reconciliation cannot be understood if Canadian history begins with Europeans.
colle: Can a settler state fully recognise Indigenous sovereignty?

title: Quebec City is founded
summary: New France gains a permanent political centre.
what: Samuel de Champlain established Quebec as a trading and administrative settlement.
why: French language, civil law and Catholic culture became enduring foundations of Quebec’s distinct identity.
colle: Is Quebec a nation within Canada?

title: The Battle of the Plains of Abraham
summary: British victory decides the fate of New France.
what: British forces defeated the French outside Quebec City during the Seven Years’ War.
why: The conquest created a bilingual and bicultural political problem that still shapes federalism.
colle: Did conquest create or merely reveal Canada’s dual identity?

title: The Royal Proclamation
summary: The Crown sets rules for Indigenous land acquisition.
what: The proclamation recognised that Indigenous lands could be ceded only to the Crown through treaties.
why: It remains a constitutional reference in Indigenous rights claims, despite repeated violations in practice.
colle: Can historical treaties constrain a modern state?

title: The War of 1812
summary: British North America resists invasion from the United States.
what: British forces, colonial militias and Indigenous allies defended the colonies during war with the US.
why: The conflict became a nation-building story, though it often underplays the decisive role and later betrayal of Indigenous allies.
colle: How are national myths created from ambiguous wars?

title: Confederation
summary: Canada becomes a federal dominion.
what: Ontario, Quebec, Nova Scotia and New Brunswick formed a federation under the British North America Act.
why: Federalism was designed to combine unity with regional and linguistic diversity.
colle: Does Canadian federalism protect diversity or produce permanent conflict?

title: The railway and the North-West Resistance
summary: Continental expansion is consolidated through force and infrastructure.
what: The Canadian Pacific Railway was completed as Métis and First Nations resistance was defeated and Louis Riel executed.
why: The year links national development to Indigenous dispossession and French-English tensions.
colle: Can nation-building be separated from colonial violence?

title: Vimy Ridge and the conscription crisis
summary: War strengthens national identity but divides French and English Canada.
what: Canadian forces captured Vimy Ridge, while compulsory military service provoked fierce opposition in Quebec.
why: The contrast shows how the same war can unite a country symbolically and divide it politically.
colle: Do wars create national unity or expose national divisions?

title: The Statute of Westminster
summary: Canada gains legislative autonomy from Britain.
what: The statute recognised the equality of self-governing dominions with the United Kingdom.
why: It marked a major stage in Canada’s gradual, peaceful path to full sovereignty.
colle: Why was Canadian independence so gradual?

title: Canadian citizenship is created
summary: Canadians receive a distinct legal nationality.
what: The Canadian Citizenship Act came into force, separating Canadian citizenship from the broader status of British subject.
why: Citizenship became a tool for defining an independent and increasingly diverse country.
colle: What should citizenship require beyond legal status?

title: First Nations people gain the federal vote without losing status
summary: A major formal barrier to political participation is removed.
what: Status First Nations adults could vote federally without being forced to give up treaty status.
why: The reform ended one discriminatory rule but did not resolve colonial control under the Indian Act.
colle: Can equal voting rights coexist with colonial institutions?

title: The Maple Leaf flag
summary: Canada adopts a distinctive national symbol.
what: The new red-and-white flag replaced the Canadian Red Ensign after a divisive parliamentary debate.
why: The flag signalled a more independent identity less centred on the British Empire.
colle: Can a new symbol successfully reinvent a country?

title: The Official Languages Act
summary: English and French receive equal status in federal institutions.
what: The Act established official bilingualism across the federal government.
why: It aimed to protect linguistic duality while provoking debate in both Quebec and western Canada.
colle: Is official bilingualism a success?

title: The October Crisis
summary: Political violence tests civil liberties.
what: After kidnappings by the FLQ, Pierre Trudeau invoked the War Measures Act and deployed troops.
why: The crisis remains a key debate about terrorism, emergency powers and Quebec nationalism.
colle: When may a democracy suspend civil liberties?

title: Official multiculturalism
summary: Canada adopts multiculturalism as federal policy.
what: The government recognised cultural diversity within a bilingual framework.
why: The “mosaic” became central to Canada’s self-image and immigration model.
colle: Does multiculturalism create inclusion or weaken a shared identity?

title: Patriation and the Charter
summary: Canada brings its constitution home.
what: The Constitution Act created a domestic amendment procedure and entrenched the Charter of Rights and Freedoms.
why: The Charter strengthened judicial review, while Quebec’s government did not consent to the agreement.
colle: Has the Charter transferred too much power to judges?

title: The Quebec referendum
summary: Canada comes within one percentage point of breaking apart.
what: Quebec voters rejected sovereignty by 50.58% to 49.42%.
why: The razor-thin result forced renewed debate over national unity, recognition and the rules of secession.
colle: Could Quebec independence return as a major issue?

title: Nunavut is created
summary: A new territory reflects Inuit self-government.
what: Nunavut was separated from the Northwest Territories following the largest Indigenous land-claim settlement in Canadian history.
why: It gave Inuit people greater political control while highlighting the continuing challenges of housing, services and Arctic sovereignty.
colle: Can territorial autonomy deliver decolonisation?

title: Residential-school apology
summary: The federal government formally apologises to former pupils.
what: The Prime Minister apologised for a system that removed Indigenous children from families and sought to erase cultures and languages.
why: The apology opened a national reckoning but did not itself repair intergenerational harm.
colle: What makes a state apology meaningful?

title: Truth and Reconciliation Commission
summary: The residential-school system is documented as cultural genocide.
what: The Commission issued 94 Calls to Action after hearing testimony from survivors.
why: Reconciliation became a central national commitment and a test of whether symbolic recognition produces structural change.
colle: Why has implementation of reconciliation been so slow?

title: Unmarked burial sites renew the reckoning
summary: Ground-penetrating radar findings intensify grief and anger.
what: Indigenous communities announced evidence of probable unmarked graves near former residential schools.
why: The discoveries made the violence of assimilation impossible for many Canadians to treat as distant history.
colle: How should schools and public institutions teach colonial violence?

title: Mark Carney becomes Prime Minister and wins a minority
summary: Leadership changes amid tensions with the United States.
what: Mark Carney was sworn in in March and the Liberals won 169 of 343 seats in the April federal election.
why: The election centred on sovereignty, economic security and Canada’s relationship with the United States.
colle: Can external pressure strengthen Canadian national unity?
